EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance prompted scattered strong to severe thunderstorms during the late evening hours across south-central Pennsylvania. Sporadic wind damage was reported with gusts up to 70 mph. Three injuries resulted at an outside event in Johnstown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cambria County emergency management and fire rescue reported multiple tents and a tower were blown down at an outside event called Thunder in the Valley. A performing stage also collapsed. The wind damage along with flying debris lead to 4 minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The runoff from heavy rain during the late morning and early afternoon caused flash flooding of smaller streams, basement and poor drainage flooding mainly across extreme eastern Pennsylvania. Flooding later that afternoon and evening occurred across a few of the larger streams and creeks. Numerous vehicles drove around barricades and motorists had to be rescued. Rain began falling during the afternoon on the 7th and did not end until the early evening on the 8th. It fell at its heaviest between 10 a.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST on the 8th. Storm totals averaged around one to three inches with the highest amounts toward New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that developed in the northeastern Gulf of Mexico on the 7th. It deepened to a 997 millibar low by the time it moved slowly northeast into West Virginia at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. At 1 p.m. EST on the 8th, there were a pair of 992 millibar lows, one near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania and the other in northern Virginia. At 4 p.m. EST on the 8th, they combined into one over southeast Pennsylvania and deepened to 987 millibars. At 7 p.m. EST the low had deepened to 985 millibars and was near New York City. Its associated cold front had cleared Delaware and New Jersey and ended the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain continued flooding into the evening along sections of the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek and the Wissahickon Creek. The Wissahickon Creek at Fort Washington was above its 9 foot flood stage from 308 p.m. EST through 719 p.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 10.53 feet at 515 p.m. EST. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Schwenksville was above its 7 foot flood stage from 514 p.m. EST through 1153 p.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 8.14 feet at 9 p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 2.26 inches in Hatboro, 2.01 inches in Pottstown and 1.76 inches in Hatfield.

Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches and melting snowfall caused flooding of some roadways, basements, and smaller creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summit Creek Road was flooded by excessive rain and snow-melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance triggered numerous thunderstorms across much of south-central Pennsylvania. One storm just east of Newport in Perry County produced a wet microburst causing significant wind damage to several homes in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team along with Perry County emergency management viewed the thunderstorm wind damage (wet microburst) in Howe Township, approximately one mile east of Newport near the intersection of Juniata Parkway East and Gypsy Hollow Road. Eight homes sustained minor damage to siding and awnings. An additional two homes had significant damage due to a tree falling on one and another with half the metal roof removed. Up to 100 trees were knocked down in an area near the Juniata River. Estimated wind speeds ranged from 65 to 90 mph. There were no injures.

The flooding occurred across northern parts of Chester County and central parts of Montgomery and Bucks Counties. Event totals averaged 1.5 inches to 2.5 inches with over an inch occurring within an hour as the thunderstorms moved through between Midnight and 2 a.m. EDT on the 20th. The thunderstorms formed along and preceding a cold front that was moving through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Event totals included 1.58 inches in Graterford and 1.55 inches in Pottstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass led to severe thunderstorms occurring across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 10th. Wind damage was reported in nearly every county in Eastern Pennsylvania. About 185,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the night of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms knocked down trees and lines in two areas of Northampton County. The first was across the northeast corridor from Bushkill Township northeast through Wind Gap, Plainfield Township and Bangor. The second area extended from Palmer Township southeast into Easton. In Palmer Township, a large tree split in half and outdoor furniture was tossed from the porch of one home. The combination of damaging winds and lightning caused about 5,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light homes and businesses to lose power in Northampton County. Metropolitan Edison reported about 12,000 homes and businesses lost power in their service area including Northampton County. All power was restored by the evening of the 11th.

Five companies helped extinguish the blaze. No injuries were reported. Another lightning strike at the Municipal Building in Whitehall Township did hardly any damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The runoff from heavy rain during the late morning and early afternoon caused flash flooding of smaller streams, basement and poor drainage flooding mainly across extreme eastern Pennsylvania. Flooding later that afternoon and evening occurred across a few of the larger streams and creeks. Numerous vehicles drove around barricades and motorists had to be rescued. Rain began falling during the afternoon on the 7th and did not end until the early evening on the 8th. It fell at its heaviest between 10 a.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST on the 8th. Storm totals averaged around one to three inches with the highest amounts toward New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that developed in the northeastern Gulf of Mexico on the 7th. It deepened to a 997 millibar low by the time it moved slowly northeast into West Virginia at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. At 1 p.m. EST on the 8th, there were a pair of 992 millibar lows, one near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania and the other in northern Virginia. At 4 p.m. EST on the 8th, they combined into one over southeast Pennsylvania and deepened to 987 millibars. At 7 p.m. EST the low had deepened to 985 millibars and was near New York City. Its associated cold front had cleared Delaware and New Jersey and ended the rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was rescued from flood waters surrounding her stranded vehicle near the Pennypack Creek in Upper Moreland Township. Flooding also started along the Wissahickon Creek in the Fort Washington area. Event precipitation totals included 2.26 inches in Hatboro, 2.01 inches in Pottstown and 1.76 inches in Hatfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team, along with the Warren County Public Safety Director, surveyed a known area of damage southwest of Clarendon along a dirt road named Farmsworth Road. Damage, in the form of trees being knocked down, were observed less than one-quarter mile along the road. Dozens of trees were knocked down parallel to the road, all pointing in the same direction for almost 1.5 miles. These trees appeared to be knocked down by a microburst as there was no evidence of tornado damage. ||Approximately one and three-quarters miles down the road, a swath of tree damage was found that appeared to be related a tornado touchdown. The tornado was approximately 250 yards long and 25 to 50 yards wide. Wind speeds were estimated at the expected value for trees being uprooted, which translates into an EF-1 tornado with a maximum wind speed approaching 90 miles per hour.||The downburst swath continued another quarter mile down Farmsworth Road which caused the total track of the downburst to be one and three-quarters miles. Wind speeds that knocked down these trees were estimated to be 70 to 80 mph. The tornado, and associated downburst winds, were part of a larger area of wind damage that extended from three miles west of Tidioute to nine miles west of Warren.||There were no injuries or deaths.

The most oppressive heat wave of the meteorological summer affected Eastern Pennsylvania from June 7th through the 10th. There were about twenty heat related deaths, eighteen within Philadelphia. There were also a couple of drownings within rivers. The combination of high temperatures well into the 90s and dew point temperatures in the 70s produced apparent temperatures or heat indices values as high as around 105 in Philadelphia and 100 to 105 elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and wind damage across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. Most of the storms developed in the afternoon, although there were a few early in the morning. Portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley experienced the strongest storms with hail over two inches in diameter and several large trees down. A few homes also sustained weather-related damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires in Newville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and wind damage across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. Most of the storms developed in the afternoon, although there were a few early in the morning. Portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley experienced the strongest storms with hail over two inches in diameter and several large trees down. A few homes also sustained weather-related damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping-pong to hen-egg size hail (1.5 to 2 inches in diameter) was reported in Mount Wolf just southeast of York Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage and large hail across portions of south-central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near the Lock Haven Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees, tree limbs and wires  across the central part of Bucks County. Downed trees were reported in Doylestown, Buckingham and Wrightstown Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front helped trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th across the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.
A cold front helped trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th across the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage across eastern Monroe County. In Stroudsburg, a tree fell onto a garage on Main Street and damaged it. In East Stroudsburg, a downed tree landed on and damaged the roof and porch of a home on Braeside Avenue. A downed tree was also reported at the Delaware Water Gap. About 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and the ensuing fire seriously damaged a farm house on the Sunny High Farm off of Pennsylvania State Route 100 in North Coventry Township. The fire began on the first floor in the front of the house. Flames were through the windows when fire fighters arrived. Five fire companies helped battle the blaze. The family dog died; no additional injuries were reported. Damage was estimated at $300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front helped trigger a couple of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th across the Poconos and Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused pockets of wind damage across the northwern tier of Northampton County from Lehigh Township east to Lower Mount Bethel Township. Trees and wires were knocked down. In Lehigh Township, in Berlinsville, shingles and gutters were torn away from a couple of homes. In Portland, a downed large oak tree destroyed the back of one porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Because of the hot and humid air mass preceding this front, some of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a group of swimmers and killed a 17-year-old male in the water at Gouldsboro State Park in Coolbaugh Township. The 17-year-old male and a 24-year-old male were in the water when lightning struck. Two juvenile female companions were along the shore and were knocked to the ground. The ladies were treated at the scene. The 24-year-old male suffered non life threatening injuries, but was briefly hospitalized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger scattered showers and thunderstorns across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. The thunderstorms themselves moved slowly and caused flash flooding in the Poconos where radar estimated storm totals reached around 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flooding of smaller streams as well as roadways along the Kidder and Penn Forest Township lines. A few roadways remained closed due to flooding into the evening of the 14th. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass led to severe thunderstorms occurring across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 10th. Wind damage was reported in nearly every county in Eastern Pennsylvania. About 185,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the night of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees and power lines across Chester County and caused one injury. About 55,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county and 18,000 still were without power the next day. A state of emergency was declared in West Vincent Township because of the numerous blocked roads. Ten roads were still closed on the 11th. In West Brandywine Township, a man was knocked unconscious after a downed tree cut a mobile home in half. In Phoenixville, a downed tree was on a house on Pothouse Road. About 80 percent of the borough lost power. Pennsylvania State Route 29 was blocked south of the borough. Roadways were also closed in East Brandywine Township and in Honeybrook (Pennsylvania State Route 10).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass led to severe thunderstorms occurring across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 10th. Wind damage was reported in nearly every county in Eastern Pennsylvania. About 185,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the night of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage from a squall line of severe thunderstorms in the northwest Philadelphia suburbs peaked in Montgomery County. A swath of wind damage  (downed trees) occurred from around Collegeville southeast into Abington Township and Hatboro. PECO Energy reported about 78,500 homes and businesses lost power, 16,000 were still without power on the evening of the 11th. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 2,000 of its customers also lost power in the county. Power was not fully restored until the 12th.||Abington, Collegeville, Royersford, Trappe and Upper Providence were among the municipalities that suffered the most damage. About 40 percent of Lansdale Borough lost power. Downed trees damaged vehicles in Whitpain and Montgomery Townships. Other municipalities that reported downed trees and some road closures included Hatboro, Pottstown, Jenkintown, Lower Gwynedd, Lower Providence and Lower Salford. The Salford Hills Elementary School dismissed children early because of lost power.||A 61-year-old man died in Upper Merion Township of carbon monoxide poisoning after running a generator in his home. His wife survived.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike knocked out power at the Mount Airy Lodge Casino Resort for fourteen hours starting the evening of the 14th. About 800 guests were evacuated from the gaming floor. Some room guests were also evacuated. The casino reopened at Noon EDT on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass led to severe thunderstorms occurring across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening of the 10th. Wind damage was reported in nearly every county in Eastern Pennsylvania. About 185,000 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the night of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front from a severe thunderstorm produced wind damage across the southern or lower part of Bucks County from Warminster and Warwick Townships east to Yardley and in locations farther to the south. The largest power outages occurred in Levittown, Lower Southampton and Newtown. PECO Energy reported about 10,000 of its customers lost power in Bucks County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dissipating but stalled frontal boundary helped trigger thunderstorms with heavy rain during the late afternoon of the 6th in Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 26th.  Severe thunderstorms developed along this front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn caught fire after a lightning strike on Devyn Drive in West Pikeland Township. The fire was contained to the barn and two nearby homes were undamaged. The historic barn though was a total loss. Six fire companies assisted in battling the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly approaching from New York State helped trigger some severe thunderstorms during the evening of the 20th in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees and poles in Cumru Township. Lightning strikes caused about 3,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Boyertown, Reading and Wyomissing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The majority of the wind and lightning damage occurred in southeastern Pennsylvania where PECO Energy reported about 35,000 homes and businesses lost power. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT through 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires across eastern Lehigh County from Coplay Borough south. In Lower Saucon Township on Seidersville Road, a downed tree limb that tore down a wire caught on fire. About 140 homes and businesses lost power in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The majority of the wind and lightning damage occurred in southeastern Pennsylvania where PECO Energy reported about 35,000 homes and businesses lost power. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT through 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21-year-old woman and a 3-year-old boy were struck and injured by lightning at the Brookside Swim and Tennis Club in Lower Makefield Township. They were running for shelter and passed under a 50-foot-tall evergreen tree when it was struck by lightning. The lightning traveled down the tree to the ground where it struck the woman. The lightning traveled up her body and exited out her arm to the boy. She suffered a first degree burn from a chain around her neck. The boy had an entrance and exit mark from the lightning and also injured his arm where we was holding onto her neck. The boy never lost consciousness, but the lady went into convulsions and lost consciousness. Both were hospitalized and survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The longest heat wave of the summer affected eastern Pennsylvania from July 16th through the 22nd. The heat wave ended on the 22nd as thunderstorms kept temperatures below 90 degrees on the 23rd. a cold front overnight then brought in drier air. High temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s throughout the heat wave. The hottest days occurred on the 18th, 19th or 20th. The combination of the temperatures and dew points produced apparent temperatures or heat indices of around 100F. There were six heat related deaths in Philadelphia. All of them were in homes that did not have air conditioning. ||The excessive heat triggered the Philadelphia Corporate of Aging to run a heat phone line in which they counseled the elderly about precautions to take against the heat and how to detect signs of heat stress. City health department nurses were available to answer questions also. It also triggered an alert to 7000 block captains throughout Philadelphia in urging relatives, neighbors and other voluntary groups to check on the elderly. The city Fire Department increased its emergency medical staffing. The Philadelphia Senior Center in conjunction with Comcast gave away three hundred window fans. In the Philadelphia suburbs, the Chester County Department of Aging Services had fans available for elderly residents requesting them. In Delaware County, a heat information line was also opened and the hours of air-conditioned senior centers were expanded. Because of poor air quality, the Suburban Link offered free rides in Chester and Montgomery Counties. ||A 17-year-old boy drowned early on July 23rd after he and three friends jumped from a railroad trestle into the Lehigh River in the Glen Onoko area of the Lehigh Gorge State Park. His body was found about six hours later.   ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Reading (Berks County) and 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Easton (Northampton County), Pottstown (Montgomery County), West Chester (Chester County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

A cold front that approached, but stalled, before passing through the region triggered a couple of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon on the 17th in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a few trees and wires in Upper Saucon Township. The combination of winds and lightning caused about 9,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Lehigh County. All power was restored by 7 a.m. EDT the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that approached, but stalled, before passing through the region triggered a couple of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon on the 17th in the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees, tree limbs and power lines across Northampton County from around Walnutport southeast into Easton. The combination of wind and lightning caused about 13,000 Pennsylvania Power and Light (PPL) and Metropolitan Edison customers to lose power. All power was restored by 7 a.m. EDT the following morning. PPL outages occurred in Lehigh and Northampton Townships as well as the city of Bethlehem. Metropolitan Edison outages occurred in Glendon, College Hill and Palmer Township. A downed tree blocked one lane of traffic along Pennsylvania State Route 946 in Lehigh Township.

His body was found about six hours later.   ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Reading (Berks County) and 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Easton (Northampton County), Pottstown (Montgomery County), West Chester (Chester County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

The longest heat wave of the summer affected eastern Pennsylvania from July 16th through the 22nd. The heat wave ended on the 22nd as thunderstorms kept temperatures below 90 degrees on the 23rd. a cold front overnight then brought in drier air. High temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s throughout the heat wave. The hottest days occurred on the 18th, 19th or 20th. The combination of the temperatures and dew points produced apparent temperatures or heat indices of around 100F. There were six heat related deaths in Philadelphia. All of them were in homes that did not have air conditioning. ||The excessive heat triggered the Philadelphia Corporate of Aging to run a heat phone line in which they counseled the elderly about precautions to take against the heat and how to detect signs of heat stress. City health department nurses were available to answer questions also. It also triggered an alert to 7000 block captains throughout Philadelphia in urging relatives, neighbors and other voluntary groups to check on the elderly. The city Fire Department increased its emergency medical staffing. The Philadelphia Senior Center in conjunction with Comcast gave away three hundred window fans. In the Philadelphia suburbs, the Chester County Department of Aging Services had fans available for elderly residents requesting them. In Delaware County, a heat information line was also opened and the hours of air-conditioned senior centers were expanded. Because of poor air quality, the Suburban Link offered free rides in Chester and Montgomery Counties. ||A 17-year-old boy drowned early on July 23rd after he and three friends jumped from a railroad trestle into the Lehigh River in the Glen Onoko area of the Lehigh Gorge State Park. His body was found about six hours later.   ||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Reading (Berks County) and 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Easton (Northampton County), Pottstown (Montgomery County), West Chester (Chester County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).

Scattered severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front moving across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. One of these severe storms produced a relatively long lived EF0 tornado in Fayette county Pennsylvania, which then moved across Preston county West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced a small EF0 tornado just east of Five Forks Pennsylvania in Fayette county, very near the Pennsylvania and West Virginia border. The tornado produced mainly tree damage in Fayette county and had a maximum width of 50 yard. Maximum winds were estimated at 60 MPH. This tornado was the first segment of a tornado that then continued southeast into Preston county West Virginia and into Garrett county Maryland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow moving thunderstorms developed under weak low pressure aloft across northern West Virginia and western Pennsylvania with some flash flooding reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported flash flooding along Belleau St in Pittsburgh closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area causing showers and thunderstorms to develop.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe, while an area of training thunderstorms produced flash flooding in northwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in less than 1.5 hours produced flash flooding in western Crawford county, closing several roadways including SR 77 near Spartansburg and SR 408 near Hydetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The majority of the wind and lightning damage occurred in southeastern Pennsylvania where PECO Energy reported about 35,000 homes and businesses lost power. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT through 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large tree limbs in Macungie. One large part of a tree damaged the roof of the former Weaver Mansion on Church Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms developed over the warm waters of Lake Erie on the morning of the 22nd.  These storms were characterized by very heavy rainfall rates, and produced flash flooding in northwestern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 to 3.5 inches fell in less than 1.5 hours across northeastern Erie County, producing flash flooding in and near the town of North East.  Basements and garages were reported flooded all over town due to the runoff, and an apartment complex was flooded by overflow from a creek in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 26th.  Severe thunderstorms developed along this front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was observed.  A few vehicles sustained damage from the hail.

One of these severe storms produced a relatively long lived EF0 tornado in Fayette county Pennsylvania, which then moved across Preston county West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny size hail northeast of Masontown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees and power lines down in Uniontown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The squall line of severe thunderstorms tore down numerous trees and wires across Berks County. Muhlenberg Township, north of Reading was hardest hit. Dozens of trees were toppled in Riverview Park. The entire length of River Road was closed. The North 5th Street Highway was closed for more than three hours because of a toppled utility pole. A few other roads were closed late into the day. A house on George Street lost a piece of its roof. A building on Leiszs Bridge Road was damaged by a downed tree. Throughout the county, about 2,500 homes and businesses lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At daybreak on September 14th, the remnants of Hurricane Ike were centered over southeastern Missouri.  This low moved rapidly northeast during the day reaching Northwest Ohio during the afternoon of the 14th.  The low then moved up Lake Erie and over Lake Huron by the late evening hours.  Damaging winds accompanied this storm system and caused widespread wind damage across northwestern Pennsylvania.  Reports of high winds and wind damage began during the evening hours and tapered off toward midnight.  The damage across the area was extensive with thousands of trees, power lines and utility poles downed.  The time of year of this event contributed greatly to the amount of damage that occurred since the trees in the area were still foliated. Many homes and buildings sustained varying degrees of damage from the high winds.  Tens of thousands of people lost power in Erie and Crawford Counties.  Some residents were without power for several days. ||Travel during this event was difficult in some areas because of the large number of downed trees, power lines and utility poles.  Power outages caused many traffic lights to be inoperable which further hampered travel.  Dozens of vehicles were damaged by fallen trees or limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ike began during the early evening hours of September 14th and continued through late evening.  Wind gusts were estimated to be around 60 mph with the strongest winds occurring between 8 and 10 pm.  A peak measured gust of 54 mph was recorded by an automated sensor at Erie International Airport at 8:29 PM EDT.  Damage in the county was extensive with hundreds of trees and many utility poles downed.  Scattered power outages occurred with some customers without power for a couple days.  Homes and buildings were damaged throughout the county.  The damage ranged from a few shingles torn off to significant structural damage caused by fallen trees landing on roofs.  Numerous vehicles were damaged by fallen trees and limbs and also from flying debris.  Substantial clean up costs were incurred by local governments.  Some of the schools in the county were forced to close on Monday the 15th because of the power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching cold front and tropical moisture that moved into the region from a decaying low pressure system over the Carolinas produced heavy rain over Eastern Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EDT and 6 p.m. EDT on the 28th. This caused flooding and flash flooding, especially in or near the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the day on the 28th led to poor drainage and roadway flooding in and around Bethlehem. The heavy rain also caused the Monocacy Creek in Bethlehem to reach flood stage during the afternoon. The Celtic Classic held in Johnston Park in Bethlehem was affected by its flooding. About six food vendors and twenty craft merchants were flooded. About 100 workers were forced to evacuate their location. That part of the park was covered by two feet of water. Performing acts were also moved to other stages. The Monocacy Creek at the gage site in Bethlehem was above its 4.5 foot flood stage from 305 p.m. EDT through 346 p.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 4.53 feet at 330 p.m. EDT. The precipitation total for the 28th from the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 3.83 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching cold front and tropical moisture that moved into the region from a decaying low pressure system over the Carolinas produced heavy rain over Eastern Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EDT and 6 p.m. EDT on the 28th. This caused flooding and flash flooding, especially in or near the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain between 330 p.m. and 530 p.m. EDT caused flooding in Radnor Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 2.5 inches. Ithan Creek rose over Iven Road in the Saint Davids area of the township. Other flooding occurred along South Wayne Avenue and West Wayne Avenue. A vehicle became stuck underneath the Radnor Trail Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching cold front and tropical moisture that moved into the region from a decaying low pressure system over the Carolinas produced heavy rain over Eastern Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. EDT and 6 p.m. EDT on the 28th. This caused flooding and flash flooding, especially in or near the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain caused the flooding and washouts of a few roads in Durham and Nockamixon Townships. A large mudslide occurred across Pennsylvania State Route 611 in Durham Township and closed it. Other major roads closed in the area included Center Hill and Kintner Hill Roads. Water poured down the embankment of Pennsylvania State Route 212 overran a house on Old Furnace Road. The water broke windows, filled the basement with water and tore away the driveway. The rush of water also scattered boulders and smaller rocks that clogged Rodges Run under two bridges on Lehnenberg Road. Pennsylvania State Route 611 was closed in both directions until the 29th. Flooding along Gallows Run and rock slides caused the road to be closed from Pennsylvania State Route 212 to Centerhill Road. In Nockamixon Township, a 170-year-old stone wall collapsed under the weight of water cascading off the steep cliffs that hug the southbound side of River Road. The rain overflowed a culvert off the cliff side of the road, washed across the roadway, filled the canal and shattered the wall. Repairs were estimated at $500,000. A homeowner on River Road in the township saw hundreds of rocks carried onto his property by a flooding stream and then onto River Road. The stream knocked down 100 feet of fencing and carried away a propane tank. The precipitation total for the 28th from the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 3.83 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike split the chimney of a shed on Poplar Street in Reading. Pieces of the chimney fell onto a car that was being parked. The driver was not hurt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper air disturbance helped trigger strong to isolated severe thunderstorms in the Lehigh Valley on the evening of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Lehigh Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper air disturbance helped trigger strong to isolated severe thunderstorms in the Lehigh Valley on the evening of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ane ensuing fire caused the displacement of residents in a two family house in North Whitehall Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure was located over northern New England. An upper trough of low pressure located over the central Great Lakes swung into the region during the afternoon hours. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region and some of them became severe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar to ping pong ball size hail fell in Fleetwood Borough. The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching secondary cold front helped trigger thunderstorms in southeast Pennsylvania on the evening of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes caused power outages and an apartment fire in eastern Montgomery County. In Montgomery Township, a lightning strike caused an apartment fire at the Avenel Apartments. The fire forced the evacuation of twenty-two apartments in the complex. The fire was contained to the kitchen area of a third floor apartment. Another lightning strike caused about 17,500 PECO Energy homes and businesses to lose power in Montgomery County. Lightning struck the North Wales substation on Sumneytown Road and tripped circuit breakers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front located in central Pennsylvania around sunrise on the 2nd helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms over Eastern Pennsylvania during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Hamilton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some isolated pulse type thunderstorms that moved across the same areas in Berks County produced falsh flooding during the afternoon of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving over the same areas of central Berks County produced flash flooding and poor drainage flooding. Creeks in Rockland Township flooded. Poor drainage flooding was reported in Reading. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 5 and 6 inches in Alsace Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Bethel Township police officer and a firefighter were struck and injured by lightning at the Bethel Township fire house on Foulk Road. Lightning struck the building and moved down along its frame. The 22-year-old male officer was standing in the open garage area when he was tossed by the lightning and knocked unconscious. The female firefighter was sitting on the bumper of a fire truck when she was struck. She suffered tingling sensations. Both were hospitalized for observations and released on the 24th. The lightning strike itself damaged the garage door opener, telephone and electronics systems in the fire house.

The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 62,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Most of the outages (about 49,000) were in PECO Energy's service area in southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Boothwyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding. Minor roadway flooding was reported throughout the Philadelphia suburbs. Some basements were flooded in the Lehigh Valley. The runoff also caused flooding along a couple of the smaller streams and creeks in Eastern Pennsylvania. Rain began during the second half of the morning of the 6th and fell at its heaviest during the afternoon and early evening. The rain ended by late that evening. Storm totals averaged from around 2 inches to around 4 inches. The lightest amounts were in the Poconos, the heaviest in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 3.66 inches in Eagleville, 3.40 inches in Wynnewood, 3.36 inches in Collegeville, 3.15 inches in Pottstown, 2.79 inches in Palm and 2.05 inches in Hatsfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Hanna brought heavy rain throughout eastern Pennsylvania and strong winds in southeastern Pennsylvania as well as some minor tidal flooding along the lower Delaware River on the 6th. Rain moved into the region during the second half of the morning, fell heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening and ended by the late evening. Rain began during the second half of the morning of the 6th and fell at its heaviest during the afternoon and early evening. The rain ended by late that evening. Storm totals averaged from around 2 inches to around 4 inches. The lightest amounts were in the Poconos, the heaviest in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. The strongest winds occurred during the afternoon and early evening. While peak wind gusts were only around 40 mph, the duration of the wind combining with the heavy rain caused weak trees and tree limbs to fall down in the southeastern part of the state. About 50,000 homes and businesses lost power. Most power was restored by the evening of the 7th. Minor tidal flooding occurred during the early evening high tide as a storm surge of around two feet moved up tidal sections of the Delaware River. Many scheduled sporting events and other outdoor activities were either cancelled or postponed. About 50 flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport were cancelled.||Southeastern Pennsylvania took the brunt of the power outages as about 43,000 homes and businesses lost power in PECO Energy's service area in and around Philadelphia. In Bucks County, about 14,000 homes and businesses lost power with most outages reported in Yardley, Bensalem, Northampton, Doylestown, Middletown and Lower Makefield. In Chester County, about 5,700 homes and businesses lost power, most in heavily wooded areas. One westbound lane of U.S. Route 422 was closed in North Coventry Township because of a downed tree. In Berks County, two homes were damaged by fallen trees: one on Hampden Boulevard in Reading and the other when a 100 foot sumac tree fell on it on South Park Road in Wyomissing. Heavy rain associated with Tropical Storm Hanna caused widespread heavy rain and poor drainage flooding. Minor roadway flooding was reported throughout the Philadelphia suburbs. Some basements were flooded in the Lehigh Valley. The runoff also caused flooding along a couple of the smaller streams and creeks in Eastern Pennsylvania. Rain began during the second half of the morning of the 6th and fell at its heaviest during the afternoon and early evening. The rain ended by late that evening. Storm totals averaged from around 2 inches to around 4 inches. The lightest amounts were in the Poconos, the heaviest in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along Frankford Creek. The creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 613 p.m. EDT through 717 p.m. EDT on the 9th. It crested at 9.27 feet at 645 p.m. EDT. Storm totals included 2.27 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.97 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport.

In spite of an unseasonably wet September, drought restrictions were tightened in a few municipalities in the Philadelphia suburbs. On September 15th, Sellersville (Bucks County) issued mandatory water restrictions. Washing cars, watering lawns, washing paved surfaces, watering trees, gardens and shrubs, filling or topping swimming pools and serving water at restaurants without a request were prohibited. The borough's reservoir was more than two-thirds empty. On September 5th, Ambler (Montgomery County) issued a mandatory drought emergency for the borough and surrounding municipalities that were served by the borough's water supply in Whitpain, Upper Dublin, Lower Gwynedd and Whitemarsh Townships. On a county weighted average for the month of September, Bucks County received 7.0 inches of rain and Montgomery County 6.9 inches of rain. Both were about 3.0 inches wetter than normal.

A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 27th. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 62,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Most of the outages (about 49,000) were in PECO Energy's service area in southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked over some large trees in the eastern part of Berks County. One large downed tree damaged a minivan and the roof of a home on Old Airport Road in Amity Township. There were about 300 weather related calls associated with the strong to severe thunderstorms in Berks County on the afternoon of the 27th. About 6,400 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 27th. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 62,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Most of the outages (about 49,000) were in PECO Energy's service area in southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees in Pottstown. One truck was struck and damaged by a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed as a weak upper level disturbance moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down on Black Creek Road and on River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The only confirmed tornado in the United States associated with Tropical Storm Hanna occurred in Allentown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum wind gusts of about 95 mph touched down in East Allentown. The tornado damaged about 50 homes and the Louis E. Dieruff High School which was closed until September 10th. About 100 trees were damaged or destroyed. The tornado touched down between North Ortt Street and East Cedar Street behind the Lehigh Valley Christian High School on Oswego Street. It proceeded to move west paralleling East Cedar Street and reached its maximum strength around North Kearney Street where EF1 damage started. The high school was within the path of the strongest part of the tornado. The tornado started to weaken just before it crossed North Irving Street and lifted around North Hart Street. ||Homes were hardest hit near the intersection of East Cedar and Kiowa Streets. Several roofs were damaged; shingles were pulled from other homes. Part of a roof was torn away from a home on North Irving Street. Along the path of the tornado siding was torn from a few other homes and power lines were pulled down in addition to the tree damage. The greatest destruction occurred on the grounds of the Louis E. Dieruff High School. The tornado peeled away a section of the high school's roof near the planetarium. This allowed the heavy rain to cause water damage to seventeen classrooms and a couple of hundred lockers. Ventilation units located on the roof were wrenched away. About twenty-five trees on the high school grounds were knocked down; the limbs damaged several school windows. Repairs to the high school were not expected to be completed until the middle of November.| |The tornado path length was about 0.6 of a mile and its maximum path width was about 30 yards. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 26-year-old Philadelphia Eagle defensive end was hospitalized after suffering from dehydration and heat exhaustion on the 29th. He was released from the hospital later that day. The high temperature at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was 87 degrees.

An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The majority of the wind and lightning damage occurred in southeastern Pennsylvania where PECO Energy reported about 35,000 homes and businesses lost power. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT through 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a 60-foot tree onto a house in Phoenixville. It toppled onto the roof, damaged the chimney and blocked the front door. Downed large tree limbs were scattered throughout Phoenixville. Power was lost for four hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 27th. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 62,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Most of the outages (about 49,000) were in PECO Energy's service area in southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of lightning strikes caused a couple of house fires and other damage across central Bucks County. One firefighter was treated for heat exhaustion. In Hilltown, a home on Paige Trail was struck by lightning on its roof. Smoke spewed from the garage section of the home. Firefighters removed a three foot by three foot section of the roof to help battle the blaze. Five fire companies assisted. The fire was contained to the attic and the second floor master bedroom of the house. In Solebury Township, the chimney of a house on Brighton Way was struck by lightning. The lightning traveled down into the basement where a fire was ignited. Four fire companies battled the blaze for two and a half hours. The fire was contained to one area of the house, but caused heavy smoke damage throughout. The house was expected to be uninhabitable for a few days. Also in Solebury Township, a lightning strike caused a transformer fire. In Warrington Township, another lightning strike damaged a home's chimney. In New Britain Township, lightning struck a PECO Energy substation. U.S. Route 202 was closed as firefighters battled the blaze. One firefighter was treated for heat exhaustion. The combination of damaging winds and lightning strikes caused about 30,000 homes and businesses to lose power in Bucks and Montgomery Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very cold air behind an arctic cold front brought significant lake effect and elevation snow showers across Garrett county Maryland, The ridges of northern West Virginia and southwest Pennsylvania, as well as northwest Pennsylvania counties closer to Lake Erie. Total snowfall over a 36 hour period was near one foot in higher elevations of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland, with 6 to 8 inches of snow in 36 hours across the lake effect counties and ridges of Pennsylvania.

A complex winter storm produced a significant ice and snow accumulation across the Laurel Highlands and North Central Mountains. Further south and east, heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding in the Lower Main Stem Susquehanna River Basin. ||The wintry precipitation caused hazardous driving conditions and many vehicle accidents were reported. Despite the ice accumulation, power outages resulting from downed trees and wires were generally isolated. A few counties reported road closures, but all interstates remained open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice accretion was observed across Clearfield County. In addition, three to seven inches of snow accumulation was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain dropped between 1.5 and 3.5 inches of rain across much of Berks County and the Poconos. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon into the early evening of the 25th. The rain caused some urban poor drainage flooding, but the recent dry weather kept streams within their banks. The Spring Street Subway flooded in Reading (Berks County). Rainfall totals included 3.60 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 3.41 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 3.28 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 2.69 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County), 2.56 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and 1.41 inches in Cornwall Terrace (Berks County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday afternoon and evening, October 28th, 2008, a frontal system stalled across the eastern seaboard, with an area of light rain across eastern Pennsylvania. A wave of low pressure began to organize along this front just off the New Jersey coast early Tuesday morning. This low pressure system rapidly intensified into a major early season winter storm as it crawled up the Hudson River Valley Tuesday and Tuesday evening. As a result, a widespread area of heavy precipitation spread across most of eastern Pennsylvania by Tuesday morning.  Northerly winds developed, which drew down colder air and resulted in a changeover to snow late Monday night over the highest elevations, with snow levels dropping to around 1000 feet in northeast Pennsylvania by late Tuesday morning.  ||Locations above 1500 feet in the Poconos received 10 to 16 inches of heavy wet snow. Across elevations between 1000 and 1500 feet, snowfall ranged from 2 to 6 inches. ||Very strong northwest winds developed across northeast Pennsylvania behind this storm. Winds gusts ranged from 40 to 50 mph in most places. In areas where there was heavy wet snow, major damage was done to trees and power lines leading to widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall measurements across the higher elevations of the county ranged |from 6 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong south winds and heavy rain caused some shallow rooted trees to fall down in Bucks County. The worst reported tree damage was in Bristol Township. The peak wind gust at the Philadelphia International Airport was 38 mph.

A strong area of low pressure moved across southern Canada on October 28th and 29th.  An arctic cold front trailing this low swept across northwestern Pennsylvania late on the 28th ushering much colder air into the region.  Northwest flow behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of the 28th.  This activity intensified during the early morning hours of the 29th.  Locally heavy snowfall was reported along with a few accidents.  The snow was very wet and heavy and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the late evening hours of October 28th.  This activity intensified during the early morning hours of the 29th and finally tapered off late in the day on the 29th.  Most of the snow showers affected the northeastern corner of the county.  A snow spotter near Spartansburg measured nearly a foot of snow, most of it falling before 8 am on the 29th.  Another spotter reported six inches of snow at Canadohta Lake. A few power outages were reported as well after the weight of the heavy snow knocked down trees, limbs and power lines.  Several accidents were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air crossing over the lower Great Lakes produced heavy lake effect snowfall across the snow belt region of northwest Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to twelve inches of lake effect snow was reported across northwest Warren County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved across southern Canada on October 28th and 29th.  An arctic cold front trailing this low swept across northwestern Pennsylvania late on the 28th ushering much colder air into the region.  Northwest flow behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the evening hours of the 28th.  This activity intensified during the early morning hours of the 29th.  Locally heavy snowfall was reported along with a few accidents.  The snow was very wet and heavy and caused scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the late evening hours of October 28th.  This activity intensified during the early morning hours of the 29th and finally tapered off late in the day on the 29th.  By 8 am on the 29th as much as 10 inches of snow had fallen on portions of southeastern Erie County.  The Corry area was especially hard hit with many accidents reported.  Scattered power outages were reported as well after the weight of the heavy snow knocked down trees, limbs and power lines.  Another few inches of snow fell during the daylight hours bringing storm totals to nearly a foot in a few areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Monday afternoon and evening, October 28th, 2008, a frontal system stalled across the eastern seaboard, with an area of light rain across eastern Pennsylvania. A wave of low pressure began to organize along this front just off the New Jersey coast early Tuesday morning. This low pressure system rapidly intensified into a major early season winter storm as it crawled up the Hudson River Valley Tuesday and Tuesday evening. As a result, a widespread area of heavy precipitation spread across most of eastern Pennsylvania by Tuesday morning.  Northerly winds developed, which drew down colder air and resulted in a changeover to snow late Monday night over the highest elevations, with snow levels dropping to around 1000 feet in northeast Pennsylvania by late Tuesday morning.  ||Locations above 1500 feet in the Poconos received 10 to 16 inches of heavy wet snow. Across elevations between 1000 and 1500 feet, snowfall ranged from 2 to 6 inches. ||Very strong northwest winds developed across northeast Pennsylvania behind this storm. Winds gusts ranged from 40 to 50 mph in most places. In areas where there was heavy wet snow, major damage was done to trees and power lines leading to widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations across the highest elevations of the county ranged from 6 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm produced a significant ice and snow accumulation across the Laurel Highlands and North Central Mountains. Further south and east, heavy rainfall resulted in minor flooding in the Lower Main Stem Susquehanna River Basin. ||The wintry precipitation caused hazardous driving conditions and many vehicle accidents were reported. Despite the ice accumulation, power outages resulting from downed trees and wires were generally isolated. A few counties reported road closures, but all interstates remained open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to eight inches of snow was reported across Potter County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving low pressure system brought a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain to north central Pennsylvania from the early morning through late afternoon. Moderate to heavy rainfall was observed across the southern tier of the Commonwealth, which caused minor flooding.||The winter weather greatly affected the morning and afternoon commute, with numerous accidents reported across the region. Periods of heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain were observed during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow and sleet was reported across Schuylkill County. A brief period of freezing rain also occurred toward the end of the event, resulting in a light ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense and rapidly developing low pressure system helped produce heavy snow over the higher terrain of the Poconos on the 28th.  A heavy precipitation band was able to chill the atmosphere to snow also over Bucks and Montgomery Counties Pennsylvania during the mid day hours on the 28th. This was an elevation driven event in the Poconos, as very little snow accumulated in the valleys. It was a dynamically cooling driven event in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs where the accumulating snow coincided with the heaviest precipitation rates. Highest accumulations in the Poconos reached 17 inches; in the northwest Philadelphia suburbs around 3.5 inches. ||In the Poconos, the rain began during the afternoon of the 27th. Over the higher terrain, precipitation changed to snow during the late evening. Precipitation fell as snow until it ended during the early evening of the 28th. The heaviest snow fell between 8 a.m. and 6 p.m. EDT on the 28th. In the valleys, precipitation type was intensity driven. When precipitation became heavier, it changed to snow, lighter it went back to rain. This meteorological process is called dynamic cooling in which so much snow is falling from aloft that the amount of heat needed to melt the snow, cools the surrounding air where it can support snow at a lower level to the point it can reach the ground. Once the snow intensity weakens, warmer surrounding air moves in and raises temperatures and the snow then melts before reaching the ground.  A similar precipitation event like this one occurred in New York State in September of 1987. This same process occurred in Bucks and Montgomery Counties around mid-day on the 28th as the rain changed to an accumulating snow for around four hours. Meanwhile precipitation started as rain the previous evening and ended as rain late in the afternoon on the 28th.||The combination of the heavy wet snow, some leaves still on trees and very strong winds led to many traveling problems as well as downed trees and wires in the Poconos. Many school districts never opened; a few others had early dismissal. A seven mile stretch of Interstate 80 was repeatedly closed between the Pennsylvania Turnpike Extension in Carbon County and Pennsylvania State Route 115 in Monroe County because of multiple tractor-trailer and vehicular accidents between 9 a.m. EDT and Noon EDT on the 28th. Many accidents were also reported on Pennsylvania State Route 940 in Monroe County. Pennsylvania State Route 423 in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) was closed. A downed large tree caused road closures in Lehighton. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 60,000 customers lost power throughout their eastern Pennsylvania service area with more than 400 separate cases. The ten thousand homes and businesses that lost power in Monroe County were concentrated in Tobyhanna and Coolbaugh Townships. An emergency shelter was opened in Coolbaugh Township. In Carbon County outages were reported in Kidder, Penn Forest and East Side. Power was not fully restored until the 30th.||In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, Bucks County took the brunt of the reported damage from the combination of the accumulating wet snow and strong winds. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power with the brunt of the outages in Warminster and Northampton Townships. Many trees still had leaves on them. Roadways were blocked by downed trees in Bristol, Langhorne and Middletown. A vehicle in West Rockhill Township skidded into a wooded area. Seven school districts cancelled after school activities. The weather forced the cancellation of a presidential campaign stop in Quakertown by United States Senator John McCain.||Snowfall totals included 17.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 7 inches in Penn Forest (Carbon County), 6 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 4.5 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 3.6 inches in Richboro (Bucks County), 3.0 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County), 2.5 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in Montgomeryville (Montgomery County) and Chalfont (Bucks County) and 1.5 inches in Fairless Hills (Bucks County) and Souderton (Montgomery County). The 17 inches that accumulated in Tobyhanna matched a record for the earliest and heaviest snowfall for the Poconos. According to the Pocono Weather Book by meteorologist Ben Gelber, a 17 inch snowfall also occurred on October 16th to 17th in Freeland (Luzerne County) in 1977.||A cold front moved through the region on the 27th and stalled offshore. A low pressure system developed on the stalled front over North Carolina on the afternoon of the 27th. It slowly moved northeast and intensified rapidly. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 28th it was just off the New Jersey Coast and had a central pressure of 1003 millibars. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 28th, the low pressure system was approaching central Long Island, New York and the pressure lowered to 995 millibars. It proceeded to move northeast and was a 989 millibar low pressure system near Hartford, Connecticut at 2 p.m. EDT on the 28th and a 986 millibar low pressure system along the northern Vermont and New Hampshire border at 8 p.m. EDT on the 28th. A closed upper level low moved through the region during the day on the 28th. The relatively colder air at its center coupled with the heavy precipitation intensity made it possible for the rain to change to snow in parts of the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the late morning and into the afternoon in Eastern Pennsylvania. The winds knocked over a tractor-trailer on the exit ramp from eastbound U.S. Route 422 to northbound U.S. Route 222 in Wyomissing (Berks County). The exit ramp was closed for two hours. Traffic was snarled in the area. The peak wind gust at Reading Regional Airport was 39 mph.

Unseasonably cold air crossing the Great Lakes lead to a prolonged period of heavy lake effect and orographically enhanced snow over the northwest mountains and western Alleghenies. The heaviest snow fell over the favored snow belt region in northwest Warren County, where two-day accumulations approached three feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations generally ranged from one to two feet. The snow belt region over the northwest portion of the county picked up as much as thirty inches. Some specific locations which reported thirty or more inches include Clarendon, Columbus and Youngsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the remains of Hurricane Ike moved across central Ohio to Lake Erie on the evening of September 14th and produced very strong winds into the early morning of September 15th across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. Widespread damage to trees and power lines was reported across the region with power outages and damage to some structures from falling trees. Power was not restored to some rural areas for one week after the storm. At the peak of the storm more than 2 million homes were without power. For a period of about 8 hours, sustained winds during the storm were from 30 to 50 MPH, with gusts well over 60 MPH. The highest reported wind gust was 81 MPH at the Beaver County Pennsylvania Airport, near the Ohio Pennsylvania state line. Total damage from the storm across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia was around 25 million dollars.

Unseasonably cold air crossing the Great Lakes lead to a prolonged period of heavy lake effect and orographically enhanced snow over the northwest mountains and western Alleghenies. The heaviest snow fell over the favored snow belt region in northwest Warren County, where two-day accumulations approached three feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations generally ranged from nine to twelve inches. The city of Bradford picked up just over a foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early-season heavy snowfall adversely affected the higher elevations of east-central and northeastern Pennsylvania, causing road closures, power outages and early dismissals from local school districts. Snowfall amounts ranged from seven inches in Sullivan County to over a foot further north and east in the Pocono mountains. However, the snowfall was very elevation dependent with little to no accumulation in the valleys. ||Numerous motor vehicle accidents were reported in Sullivan County, although none were serious according to state police in Laporte. Sections of U.S. Route 220 were temporarily closed due to a jackknifed tractor trailer and other trucks that were stuck in the snow. Other roads in the Lopez area were also briefly shut down. ||The accumulation of the heavy, wet snow on power lines and trees resulted in approximately 1,200 Penelec customers to be without power at the height of the storm. Schools were dismissed early due to the loss of power and deteriorating road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative observer in Laporte measured seven inches of snow, which fell from the early morning hours into the mid afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold northwest flow over the Great Lakes produced multiple bands of lake effect snow showers across much of western Pennsylvania. This long duration event lasted from late on the 28th into the evening of the 29th and was the first lake-effect snow accumulation of the 2008-2009 winter season. ||A few intense snow bands produced several inches of snow in a short duration. A general one to three inch snowfall was observed across much of the northern and western mountains of central Pennsylvania, with more localized amounts in excess of six inches recorded over the snow belt region of western Warren County and the highest elevations in the Laurel Highlands of Somerset County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS snowfall observer a few miles southeast of St. Mary's reported nine inches. The Cooperative observer in Ridgway reported approximately seven inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure from the remains of Hurricane Ike moved across central Ohio to Lake Erie on the evening of September 14th and produced very strong winds into the early morning of September 15th across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania. Widespread damage to trees and power lines was reported across the region with power outages and damage to some structures from falling trees. Power was not restored to some rural areas for one week after the storm. At the peak of the storm more than 2 million homes were without power. For a period of about 8 hours, sustained winds during the storm were from 30 to 50 MPH, with gusts well over 60 MPH. The highest reported wind gust was 81 MPH at the Beaver County Pennsylvania Airport, near the Ohio Pennsylvania state line. Total damage from the storm across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia was around 25 million dollars.

Unseasonably cold northwest flow over the Great Lakes produced multiple bands of lake effect snow showers across much of western Pennsylvania. This long duration event lasted from late on the 28th into the evening of the 29th and was the first lake-effect snow accumulation of the 2008-2009 winter season. ||A few intense snow bands produced several inches of snow in a short duration. A general one to three inch snowfall was observed across much of the northern and western mountains of central Pennsylvania, with more localized amounts in excess of six inches recorded over the snow belt region of western Warren County and the highest elevations in the Laurel Highlands of Somerset County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warren County PennDOT reported snowfall accumulations of approximately ten to twelve inches across much of western Warren County. Numerous vehicle accidents resulted in several road closures countywide. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry August led to a couple of municipalities in Bucks County to issue watering restrictions. Richland Township issued a drought warning that prohibited the use of automatic sprinklers within the township. On August 21st, Sellersville issued voluntary water restrictions asking residents to cut water usage by ten percent. Borough reservoirs were only thirty-three percent full. On August 27th, Doylestown asked residents to cut back water usage by ten percent. Well water levels were dropping, but did not reach drought stage yet.||August 2008 was an unseasonably dry month throughout eastern Pennsylvania. On a county weighted average, Bucks County was the driest with only 1.3 inches of precipitation, 3.1 inches less than normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought warning.

A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gust front associated with the squall line of severe thunderstorms tore down a few trees and wires in Delaware County, mainly in the northwestern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a large tree and several large limbs on Horseshoe Road in West Norriton Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front helped develop a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down trees at the intersection of Koerner Road and Pennsylvania State Route 390 in Paradise Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest flow over the Great Lakes produced numerous lake effect snow showers across the northwest mountains of central Pennsylvania. An intense band set up along the Pennsylvania New York border during the evening and overnight hours from the 16th into the 17th. Snowfall amounts were highly variable and localized in nature, ranging from as little as a few inches to over a foot in extreme northwest Warren county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warren county Emergency Management and PENN DOT officials reported a general six to twelve inch accumulation across the extreme northwest portion of the county. Localized amounts in this area exceeded a foot, with Bear Lake picking up sixteen to twenty inches.

An unusually strong for August upper air disturbance helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania. Severe thunderstorms started developing along the warm front in the morning and continued through the afternoon and into the evening until a cold front moved through the eastern part of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Weisenberg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed once again ahead of a slow moving cold front across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees down in Ohiopyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold frontal passage coupled with an upper air disturbance aloft helped cause flash flooding in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused stream, street and poor drainage flooding in Hamilton Township. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 5 and 6 inches in the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an upper level disturbance in northwest flow aloft across the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel size hail near the intersection of |I-79 and I-80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding along the Bucks and Montgomery County border on the evening of the 4th. The runoff from the heavy rain later caused flooding along the Neshaminy Creek. Precipitation event totals reached around two inches, most of which fell within an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams as well as poor drainage flooding across central and lower Bucks Counties. In Warrington Township, Street and Folly Roads were closed. Flooding and road closures also occurred along Taylorsville Road in Lower Makefield Township and Second Street Pike in Southampton Township. Poor drainage flooding was also reported along Indian Creek Drive in Bristol Township. Event precipitation totals included 2.21 inches in Doylestown, 1.99 inches in Washington Crossing and 1.64 inches in Neshaminy Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and isolated wind damage across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Chambersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved south across the eastern Great Lakes and combined with daytime heating produced thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. Isolated flash flooding and severe thunderstorms occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding along Millers Run Rd near  Treveskyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS formed along a stalled frontal boundary across the Ohio Valley.  These severe thunderstorms produced significant wind damage across parts of eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported large trees and power lines down north of Clymer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed south into the area and stalled, as a strong low pressure system moved northeast along the boundary. While areas north and along the frontal boundary received mixed precipitation, heavy rain fell to the south. Combined with runoff from existing snow pack, flash flooding was experienced across parts of northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide blocked the road across Route 220 in Monroe Township and closed the road at that location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front that ended a week long heat wave also triggered severe thunderstorms across eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and early evening on the 23rd. The majority of the wind and lightning damage occurred in southeastern Pennsylvania where PECO Energy reported about 35,000 homes and businesses lost power. A power outage caused significant delays and over a one hour shut down (from 430 p.m. EDT through 545 p.m. EDT) of Amtrak train service between Philadelphia and Boston. Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed at Philadelphia International Airport.

Five SEPTA Regional Rail Lines were also affected by the outage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike created a three foot hole in the roof of the Allen Township Public Works building. The trusses of the building were not damaged. A canvas was placed over the hole until it was repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across central Pennsylvania associated with a cold front crossing the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Russell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across the Commonwealth during the afternoon and evening hours. This resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and utility lines. There were no injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clinton County emergency management reported trees down in Mill Hall and Beech Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms affected a small portion of south-central Pennsylvania during the evening hours. Large hail and wind damage was reported in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adams County emergency management reported trees and wires down in Biglerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms affected central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 16, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Stewardson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that accompanied a cold frontal passage produced wind damage across Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 8th. The combination of the severe storms and the strong winds which followed caused about 170,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Power was not fully resored until the night of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe showers and thunderstorms caused wind damage across Montgomery County. The worst reported damage occurred in Norristown. The wind blew off part of an apartment building roof and forced fourteen residents from their apartments. The roof landed on a car dealership. Downed trees closed roads for up to two days in Upper Pottsgrove, West Pottsgrove and West Conshohocken. Numerous trees were knocked down in Lower Merion Township. A wind gust to 65 mph was measured at the Willow Grove NAS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage triggered severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 27th. The combination of lightning and damaging winds caused about 62,000 homes and businesses to lose power. Most of the outages (about 49,000) were in PECO Energy's service area in southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several trees in Eagleville in Lower Providence Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across northeast Pennsylvania in a hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front. Many of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.
